Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 703 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Tarun Hotchand Chainani

Facts.Text: Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1999 by Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh as equal shareholders.

Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd was incorporated in 2004 by Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh, who transferred their respective shares in Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to it and remained equal shareholders.
Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd wholly owns Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.
Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh were the only two directors of both Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1999 and was in the business of general wholesale trade, in particular, of electronic products and mobile phones.

Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh were former schoolmates who decided to start a business venture trading in consumer electronics and incorporated the Company in November 1999 for that purpose.
Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh are equal shareholders of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, which wholly owns Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

In or around 2005, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh arrived at an understanding to use the Company's funds to invest in stock and/or real estate on behalf of the Company, to account to each other and the Company for principal sums and profits, and to distribute profits equally between them as equal shareholders of the Holding Company.

From around 2005, Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh used the Company's funds to invest in ten properties and conducted themselves in accordance with the Understanding in respect of these investments.
Mandarin Gardens was sold in late 2012 and sale proceeds of S$295,496.97 were received by Mr Singh on completion of the sale.
Evelyn Road was sold in November 2014.
Meadows was sold in August 2015.
Spring Grove was sold in August 2016.
Parc Olympia was sold in January 2017.
Archipelago was sold in December 2017.
Spottiswoode was sold in July 2019.
Burj Vista was sold in July 2013.
FV-21-2105, The Hills 1401, The Hills 505 and Mira were sold in 2014, and The Hills 702, The Hills 1204, BD Blvd 44 and BD Blvd 39 have also been sold.

The minutes of a meeting of the Company's board of directors on 19 November 2009, signed by Mr Chainani and Mr Singh, record that the directors resolved that an interim dividend of S$1.5m for the year ending 31 December 2009 would be paid to the Company's shareholders, the Holding Company.
On 20 November 2009, the Company issued a notice to the Holding Company advising that the Dividend was declared and payable to the Holding Company.
Sometime in 2005, the Company issued 500,000 new shares at S$1 per share and its total number of shares increased to 1m shares.
In 2008, the Company issued 1m new shares at S$1 per share and its total number of shares increased to 2m shares.

In financial statements for the year ending 31 December 2007, the Company recorded an amount due from the Holding Company of S$502,800, of which S$500,000 was owed for the Company's 500,000 new shares in 2005.
In financial statements for the year ending 31 December 2008, the Company recorded an amount due from the Holding Company of US$1,061,067.
In 2009, amounts due from directors, including US$450,935.10 from Mr Chainani and S$55,398 from Mr Singh, were transferred to the Holding Company's books.

In financial statements for the year ending 31 December 2009, the Company recorded an amount due from the Holding Company of US$1,566,618 and reflected the declaration of a S$1.5m dividend to the Holding Company.
In financial statements for the year ending 31 December 2009 and 31 December 2010, the Holding Company's financial statements reflected the Dividend and receipt of the Dividend in 2009.
In 2009 and 2010, the Company and the Holding Company declared dividends, and the dividends declared by the Company were used to set off amounts owed by the Holding Company to the Company.
In 2010, the Company and the Holding Company declared dividends, and the financial statements reflected the Dividend and related intercompany balances.

The Company's Journal Entry No 30409 dated 1 January 2011 contained a credit entry described as "AUDIT ENTRIES 2010" for US$1,634,217.17 in relation to Mr Singh's account, reducing the cumulative balance from US$1,119,099.44 on 31 December 2010 to a deficit of US$515,117.73 on 1 January 2011.
By 31 December 2011, doubtful receivables totalling US$607,678.10 had been transferred to Mr Singh's loan ledger, and a tax credit of US$81,000 was recorded.
Mr Chainani signed off on the financial statements of the Company and the Holding Company every year until the year ending 2015.

In 2017, Mr Chainani removed himself as a co-signatory for the Company's Standard Chartered Bank account because he decided to transfer his shares in the Company to Mr Singh, although that did not materialise.
In 2017, Mr Chainani informed Mr Singh that no moneys were supposed to be removed from the Company's Standard Chartered Bank account without his consent.
The 14 December 2015 Ledger is Mr Singh's ledger account with the Company from 30 June 2008 to 14 December 2015.
The 12 April 2019 Ledger is Mr Singh's ledger account with the Company from 30 June 2008 to 12 April 2019.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the commencement of the dispute, the pattern of using Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd funds for property and share investments, combined with the handling of intercompany balances, director ledgers, and dividend declarations, created a situation in which the plaintiff’s economic interest as an equal ultimate owner of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd would be affected by how those investments and related cash flows were recorded, applied, and reported.
Party_Details.Rule: In shareholder disputes involving closely held companies, courts generally assess whether pre-dispute conduct in managing company assets and recording intercompany and director transactions aligns with the parties’ agreed investment arrangements and with company law requirements on share subscriptions, dividends, and maintenance of proper accounts, in order to determine if relief such as winding up or an order for accounts is appropriate.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute facts Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh jointly set up Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, used company funds for multiple property and share investments on an agreed basis, and recorded substantial intercompany and director balances, including share subscriptions, a S$1.5 million dividend, and a large audit journal entry affecting the director ledger of Avinderpal Singh. The plaintiff repeatedly requested detailed accounts for specific investments and for Dubai properties, and received partial spreadsheets and ledger extracts. These circumstances link the plaintiff’s position as an equal ultimate owner of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd to the way in which company funds were deployed into investments and how those transactions were documented and reconciled between the operating company, the holding company, and the directors’ ledgers.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court characterised the action as a shareholder oppression claim under s 216 of the Companies Act. It analysed whether the companies functioned as a quasi-partnership and concluded that, although that label applied to the relationship between the two individuals, the decisive element was the specific commercial understanding between them. The judge identified an informal understanding from around 2005 that company funds would be used to invest in stocks and real estate on behalf of the company, that the investing individual would account for principal and profits to the company and to the other individual, and that profits would ultimately be shared equally through the holding company. The judge held that this understanding applied to all the listed Singapore and overseas properties and share counters, including BD Blvd Point 39-3901, and that the properties were to be treated as acquired on behalf of the company to the extent company funds were used.

On that basis, the judge concluded that Avinderpal Singh owed duties to account both to the company and to the plaintiff for principal sums and profits from those investments. The court found that, from about 2013 onwards, he persistently failed to provide proper accounts despite repeated requests, instead giving delayed, incomplete, or opaque spreadsheets. This conduct was treated as a clear and continuing failure to comply with the agreed accounting arrangement. The judge rejected the argument that the plaintiff’s access to company records or the existence of some spreadsheets amounted to a discharge of the duty to account.

In assessing oppression, the court applied the standard of commercial unfairness under s 216, focusing on the parties’ legitimate expectations arising from their informal agreement. It held that the plaintiff, as a 50% shareholder of the holding company and ultimate owner of the operating company, was entitled to expect that investments made with company funds under the understanding would be properly accounted for. The persistent failure to account was found to be a visible departure from that expectation and therefore commercially unfair conduct in the affairs of the holding company, taking into account that the operating company was its sole asset. The court rejected the contention that only minority shareholders can invoke s 216, noting that a 50% shareholder without unilateral control can also seek relief.

The judge distinguished between personal oppression claims and corporate wrongs. Allegations that director loans or other payments recorded in the 2015 and 2019 ledgers were unauthorised, and that the large US$1.634 million audit entry and related bad-debt transfers were improper, were treated as potential wrongs to the company rather than to the plaintiff personally. Applying the reasoning in Suying Design and Sakae, the court held that any misapplication of company funds or misstatement of director balances would primarily injure the company, and that such matters should be pursued by or on behalf of the company, for example by a liquidator, rather than as oppression. The same analysis was applied to the S$1.5 million dividend: the court accepted the explanation that the dividend was declared to the holding company and set off against amounts due for share subscriptions and transferred director balances, and noted that the plaintiff had signed the relevant financial statements. It found no separate act of commercial unfairness to the plaintiff in relation to the dividend or the US$1.6 million entry.

Having found commercial unfairness only in relation to the failure to account for investments under the understanding, the court turned to remedies under s 216. It considered that both the holding company and the operating company were dormant and that the operating company functioned as a vehicle for the holding company and its two equal owners. Relying on the breadth of s 216(2) and (3) and on authorities such as Kumagai Gumi and Ng Kek Wee, the judge concluded that winding up both companies was an appropriate way to bring the unfairness to an end and to allow a neutral officeholder to deal with any corporate claims, including possible misapplication of funds or director loans.

The court ordered that both Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd be wound up and indicated that liquidators would be appointed, with consequential directions to follow. It further ordered that an account be taken by Avinderpal Singh of all principal sums of company funds used to acquire stocks and real estate under the understanding and of all profits from those investments. The judge rejected attempts to exclude particular properties or shares from the scope of the account at this stage, holding that such objections should be addressed in the accounting process. The court declined to treat the director loan ledgers, the US$1.6 million audit entry, and the S$1.5 million dividend as separate oppression grounds, leaving any remedies for those matters to be pursued as corporate claims in the winding-up process. Overall, the plaintiff’s oppression claim was allowed to the extent of the findings on the understanding, the failure to account, and the grant of winding-up and accounting relief.

Metadata.Coram: General Division of the High Court — Suit No 703 of 2020
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: General Division of the High Court of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh

Facts.Text: Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1999 by Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh as equal shareholders.

Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd was incorporated in 2004 by Tarun Hotchand Chainani and Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh, who transferred their respective shares in Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd to the Holding Company and remained equal shareholders in the Holding Company.
Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh was one of the two directors of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.
Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh was one of the two directors of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd.
Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani were equal shareholders and co-directors of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.
Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani were equal shareholders and co-directors of Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd, which wholly owns Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd.

In or around 2005, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani entered into an understanding to use the Company's funds to invest in stock and/or real estate on behalf of the Company, pursuant to which they were to account to each other and to the Company for the principal sums invested and the profits made from such investments, with such profits to be distributed equally between them as equal shareholders of the Holding Company.

From around 2005, Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and Tarun Hotchand Chainani used the Company's funds to invest in ten properties and conducted themselves in accordance with the Understanding in respect of those investments.
Mandarin Gardens was sold in late 2012 and sale proceeds of S$295,496.97 were received by Mr Singh on completion of the sale.
On 28 September 2013, Mr Singh replied by email that '200k+ was the property tax incurred and as of now no recourse; funds had deposited in HSBC; but wil chk n revert'.
Evelyn Road was sold in November 2014.

On 4 December 2017, Mr Singh sent Mr Chainani a spreadsheet purporting to account for the Evelyn Road transaction, which showed that his wife, Ms Simrit Kaur Dang, was given S$40,000 and that S$37,222 was paid to Myanma Food For Thought Pte Ltd, without explanation.
Meadows was sold in August 2015.
Spring Grove was sold in August 2016.

On 23 August 2016, Ms Callang sent Mr Chainani an email purporting to set out payments done from the proceeds of the sale of Spring Grove, including entries for AVITAR, MFFT, HSBC CC, MARINA ONE RES, SWIMMING CLUB, POSB TOP UP and CIMB FT, and showing a balance of 71,022.
Parc Olympia was sold in January 2017.
Archipelago was sold in December 2017.
Spottiswoode was sold in July 2019.
Burj Vista was sold in July 2013.
FV-21-2105, The Hills 1401, The Hills 505 and Mira were sold in 2014, and The Hills 702, The Hills 1204, BD Blvd 44 and BD Blvd 39 have also been sold.
On 15 July 2015, Mr Chainani asked Mr Singh via WhatsApp to remind Mr Davinderpal to provide 'the accounts for the Dubai properties', and Mr Singh responded 'Ok'.
On 21 July 2016, Mr Chainani requested via WhatsApp that Mr Singh 'please email the Dubai accounts'. Mr Singh replied that he was 'chasing' Mr Davinderpal.
On 14 September 2016, Ms Callang sent Mr Chainani the 14 September 2016 Dubai Spreadsheet, which referred to eight Dubai properties, being all the Dubai properties save for Burj Vista.
On 15 September 2016, Mr Singh replied 'Wil chk when back'.
On 10 July 2018 and 31 July 2018, Mr Singh sent Mr Chainani updated spreadsheets referring to the eight Dubai properties.
On 10 July 2018, Mr Singh sent Mr Chainani a spreadsheet setting out payments made in 2014, 2015 and 2016 for the purchase of Marina 19 and Marina 20.

Burj Vista was sold in July 2013; FV-21-2105, The Hills 1401, The Hills 505 and Mira were sold in 2014; The Hills 702, The Hills 1204, BD Blvd 44 and BD Blvd 39 have also been sold; and Marina 19 and Marina 20 were presently unsold.
On 19 November 2009, the directors resolved that an interim dividend of S$1.5m for the year ending 31 December 2009 would be paid to the Company's shareholders, the Holding Company.
On 20 November 2009, the Company issued a notice to the Holding Company advising that the Dividend was declared and payable to the Holding Company, signed by Mr Singh.
Sometime in 2005, the Company issued 500,000 new shares at S$1 per share and the total number of shares increased to 1m shares.
In 2008, the Company issued 1m new shares at S$1 per share and its total number of shares increased to 2m shares.
In the financial statements for the year ending 31 December 2007, the amount due from the Holding Company was S$502,800, of which S$500,000 was owed for the 2005 share issue.
In 2008, the amount due from the Holding Company to the Company increased to US$1,061,067.

In 2009, Mr Chainani's Director's Company debt of US$450,935 and Mr Singh's Director's Company debt of S$55,398 were transferred to the Holding Company's books, increasing the amount due from the Holding Company to the Company to US$1,566,618.

In 2009, the Company declared a dividend of S$1.5m, reducing the amount due from the Holding Company to the Company to about US$0.498m, as reflected in the financial statements for the year ending 31 December 2009.

The Holding Company's financial statements for the years ending 31 December 2009 and 2010 reflected that the Dividend was received by the Holding Company in 2009, and Mr Chainani signed off on both sets of financial statements as a director of the Holding Company.
In 2009 and 2010, the Company and the Holding Company declared dividends, and the dividends declared by the Company were used to set off amounts owed by the Holding Company to the Company.
The Company and the Holding Company declared dividends in 2009 and 2010, and the dividends declared by the Company were used to set off amounts owed by the Holding Company to the Company.

The Company's Journal Entry No 30409 dated 1 January 2011 contained a credit entry described as 'AUDIT ENTRIES 2010' for US$1,634,217.17 in relation to Mr Singh's account, reducing the cumulative balance from US$1,119,099.44 on 31 December 2010 to a deficit of US$515,117.73 on 1 January 2011.
The 14 December 2015 Ledger and the 12 April 2019 Ledger are Mr Singh's ledger accounts with the Company from 30 June 2008 to those dates respectively.
The 12 April 2019 Ledger is Mr Singh's ledger account with the Company from 30 June 2008 to 12 April 2019.

In 2017, Mr Chainani removed himself as a co-signatory for the Company's bank account with Standard Chartered Bank because he decided to transfer his shares in the Company to Mr Singh, although that did not materialise.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the commencement of the dispute, Avinderpal Singh’s handling of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd’s funds for property and share investments, his management of director loan ledgers and intercompany balances, and his responses to repeated requests for detailed accounts created a conflict between his role in managing company funds and the equal economic position of the other ultimate owner.
Party_Details.Rule: In disputes involving closely held companies, a director who manages company funds and undertakes investments on behalf of the company is generally maintain accurate records, explain significant ledger entries and intercompany transfers, and provide coherent accounts of investment-related cash flows, so that the economic interests of all owners are properly reflected and can be verified.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh controlled or directed the use of Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd funds for multiple property and share investments, including Singapore and Dubai properties and listed shares, and that he was centrally involved in recording director loans, intercompany balances, and a large audit journal entry affecting his director ledger. He participated in share issuances and a S$1.5 million dividend declaration that were used to adjust balances between the operating company, the holding company, and the directors. Over several years, the other equal owner requested detailed accounts for specific investments and for Dubai properties, and Avinderpal Singh responded with partial spreadsheets and explanations, while some transactions, such as the US$1.634 million audit entry and the treatment of doubtful receivables, lacked contemporaneous explanation. These circumstances frame the later judicial assessment of whether his conduct in managing and documenting company funds and investments was consistent with the expectations arising from the parties’ investment arrangement and their equal ownership structure.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log:
The court accepted that Avinderpal Singh s/o Ranjit Singh and the plaintiff had an informal understanding from around 2005 that company funds would be used to invest in stocks and real estate on behalf of the company, that the investing individual would account for principal and profits to the company and to the other individual, and that profits would ultimately be shared equally through the holding company. The judge held that this understanding applied to all the listed properties and share counters, including BD Blvd Point 39-3901, and that, to the extent company funds were used, the assets were to be treated as held on behalf of the company. On that basis, the court concluded that Avinderpal Singh owed duties to account both to the company and to the plaintiff for principal sums and profits from those investments.

Reviewing the chronology, the judge found that from about 2013 onwards Avinderpal Singh persistently failed to provide proper accounts despite repeated requests. The court noted that he delayed in providing information after sales of properties such as Mandarin Gardens, Evelyn Road, Meadows @ Pierce, Spring Grove, Parc Olympia, Archipelago, Spottiswoode, and various Dubai properties. When he did respond, he often supplied spreadsheets that lacked clear linkage between purchase prices, sale prices, expenses, and transfers back to the company, and that were unsupported by underlying documents. The judge considered that these spreadsheets did not amount to proper accounts under the agreed arrangement. The court also observed that more structured explanations, including for the Dubai properties and Marina Way units, appeared only in his affidavit of evidence-in-chief filed in 2023, long after the investments and after the dispute had arisen.

The judge rejected Avinderpal Singh’s argument that the plaintiff’s access to company records or the existence of some spreadsheets discharged his duty to account. The court emphasised that, under the understanding, the individual who conducted the investments was expected to provide coherent accounts, not merely leave the other owner to reconstruct transactions from raw records. The judge also rejected the suggestion that the understanding did not apply to certain properties or shares, holding that his admissions in pleadings and submissions bound him to treat all the listed properties and shares as falling within the arrangement.

In assessing oppression, the court applied the commercial unfairness standard under s 216, focusing on the legitimate expectations arising from the understanding. It held that the plaintiff, as an equal shareholder of the holding company and ultimate owner of the operating company, was entitled to expect that investments made with company funds under the understanding would be properly accounted for. The persistent failure by Avinderpal Singh to provide adequate accounts was found to be a clear departure from that expectation and therefore commercially unfair conduct in the affairs of the holding company. The court rejected his contention that only minority shareholders can invoke s 216, noting that a 50% shareholder without unilateral control can also seek relief.

The judge distinguished these findings from allegations about director loans, the US$1.634 million audit entry, and the S$1.5 million dividend. For those matters, the court accepted that Avinderpal Singh had provided an explanation: director balances were transferred from the operating company to the holding company, doubtful receivables were moved into his director ledger and written off, and the dividend was declared and set off against amounts due for share subscriptions and transferred director balances. The court held that any impropriety in those transactions would primarily constitute a wrong to the company rather than to the plaintiff personally, and therefore should be addressed as corporate claims, for example by a liquidator, rather than as oppression under s 216.

Having found that Avinderpal Singh’s failure to account for investments under the understanding amounted to commercial unfairness, the court considered appropriate relief. It noted that both the holding company and the operating company were dormant and that the operating company functioned as a vehicle for the holding company and its two equal owners. Relying on the broad remedial powers under s 216 and on authorities such as Kumagai Gumi and Ng Kek Wee, the judge ordered that both Avitar Enterprises Pte Ltd and Avitar Holdings Pte Ltd be wound up, with liquidators to be appointed and consequential directions to follow. The court also ordered that an account be taken by Avinderpal Singh of all principal sums of company funds used to acquire stocks and real estate under the understanding and of all profits from those investments. It declined to exclude particular properties or shares from the scope of the account at this stage, holding that any disputes about whether company funds were used should be resolved in the accounting process. The court did not grant oppression relief in relation to the director loan ledgers, the US$1.6 million audit entry, or the S$1.5 million dividend, leaving any remedies for those matters to be pursued as corporate claims in the winding-up process. On this basis, the court held Avinderpal Singh liable in the oppression action and granted winding-up and accounting relief against him.
